primary_diagnosis.name: Acute adrenal (Addisonian) crisis precipitated by intercurrent illness and missed hydrocortisone

pathophysiology_summary: In established primary adrenal insufficiency the adrenal cortex cannot mount a cortisol (and aldosterone) response to physiological stress. An intercurrent illness sharply increases glucocorticoid requirement at the same time that vomiting prevents absorption of the usual oral hydrocortisone, so circulating cortisol collapses. Cortisol deficiency causes loss of vascular tone and catecholamine responsiveness (refractory hypotension), impaired free-water excretion and mineralocorticoid deficiency (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, volume depletion), and hypoglycaemia risk; the result is distributive/hypovolaemic shock with altered mental status. Definitive treatment is immediate parenteral hydrocortisone plus IV volume resuscitation with normal saline; delay progresses to irreversible shock, coma and death. Oral steroid is inadequate once the patient is vomiting or shocked.

differential.evidence_basis: Known Addison's disease + missed steroid doses during a vomiting illness + profound weakness and shock is the textbook precipitant-and-presentation for adrenal crisis.
The preceding stomach bug and vomiting invite an anchoring trap; but the degree of shock and confusion is disproportionate to two vomits.
Fever 38.2 and hypotension; infection is itself a recognised trigger of adrenal crisis, so the two can coexist and must be covered.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the combination of known adrenal insufficiency and missed steroids during illness should immediately raise adrenal crisis as leading, not a simple gastro.

differential.evidence_basis: Refractory hypotension, altered mental status and inability to stand, disproportionate to the mild GI prodrome, in a steroid-dependent patient who has missed doses.
Cannot be excluded remotely; fever present. Managed in parallel — does not change the immediate need for parenteral hydrocortisone and 000.
Normal capillary glucose (5.4 mmol/L) and no diabetes history — osmotic diuresis is not the cause of the hypovolaemia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Sepsis stays on the list as a co-trigger, but must not delay adrenal-crisis treatment; DKA is reasonably excluded by a normal glucose.

differential.evidence_basis: Whole picture: steroid-dependent patient, missed doses during vomiting illness, refractory hypotension, altered mental status, dehydration — a time-critical endocrine emergency.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive confirmation (electrolytes, cortisol, ACTH stimulation) is a hospital/lab process; telehealth reaches a strong presumptive diagnosis that mandates emergency treatment now.

red_flags.description: Refractory hypotension (systolic ~85) unresponsive to oral fluids in a steroid-dependent patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distributive/hypovolaemic shock of adrenal crisis; requires parenteral hydrocortisone and IV saline within minutes.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to irreversible circulatory collapse and death.

red_flags.description: Progressive drowsiness and confusion (altered mental status)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Impending coma from hypotension, hyponatraemia and possible hypoglycaemia; airway and neurological deterioration risk.

red_flags.description: Missed maintenance hydrocortisone during a vomiting illness (no stress dosing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The direct precipitant — the definitive treatment (parenteral steroid) has not been delivered; every minute of delay worsens shock.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to give steroid is the fatal error; oral replacement will not absorb while vomiting.

red_flags.description: Shock and confusion disproportionate to a mild gastrointestinal prodrome
red_flags.clinical_significance: The disproportion is the clue that points from simple gastroenteritis to endocrine failure — the anti-anchoring signal.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on gastroenteritis and discharging home is a fatal under-triage.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the whole picture to the preceding stomach bug and treating it as viral gastroenteritis/dehydration, missing that the shock and confusion are disproportionate and that a steroid-dependent patient has missed doses.
Advising the patient to just take (or double) their oral hydrocortisone tablets — these will not absorb while vomiting and shocked; the crisis needs parenteral steroid and cannot wait.
Framing this as ordinary dehydration correctable with oral rehydration, ignoring the hyponatraemia/hyperkalaemia physiology and the need for steroid.
Treating adrenal crisis and infection as mutually exclusive; fever means concurrent infection must be covered in hospital, but it never delays the steroid or the ambulance.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Systolic ~85 mmHg, HR ~118, altered mental status — decompensated shock
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Immediate emergency resuscitation pathway; not amenable to remote management.

cannot_determine_via_chat: True blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured); the home-cuff reading is unverified
Morning serum cortisol and the ACTH (synthetic corticotropin) stimulation test response
Degree of dehydration on physical examination and true postural drop
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance now; if a home emergency hydrocortisone injection kit is available, instruct the carer to give it (100 mg IM) while awaiting paramedics; lie the patient flat with legs elevated; do not rely on oral steroid or oral fluids.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low risk of over-calling; the dominant risk is UNDER-action — mislabelling as gastroenteritis/dehydration and failing to escalate or to instruct the emergency hydrocortisone injection.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency (2016)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: In suspected adrenal crisis, give parenteral hydrocortisone (100 mg IM/IV) immediately with IV isotonic saline resuscitation; do not delay treatment for confirmatory testing.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Adrenal insufficiency sick-day rules (patient emergency guidance)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: During illness with vomiting, oral steroid cannot be relied upon; an emergency intramuscular hydrocortisone injection and urgent hospital care are required.
